author: catwith1hat
content: For all state divergence items in the repo, there is test code to reproduce that. All of those should probably go into some test suits

author: potuz

author: felix314159_
content: hi, first of all thanks for trying to help secure ethereum 🙂 as far as i understand, the issues in your repo that were a problem on live mainnet have already been fixed, so there is no need for secrecy? if so, it would be best if you could clone https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs and have your agent read `.claude/commands` (a set of skills that can help you write actual test cases we can run against clients using our tooling) and then make a  PR with a minimal test for each confirmed issue you have found. if you struggle at any point feel free to ask, we actively try to make the repo more first-time-contributor friendly

author: catwith1hat
content:
<@510888004366368794> I made the repo public :). I'll see what I can get going. I was hoping to get some feedback first on: "that makes sense" or "that's all wrong" first. 

For glamsterdam, I was hoping that the devs can have a look first, because I will be slow and bal-devnet-7 is coming soon.

author: jochembrouwer
content:
I like this approach, am I correct that there are 3 bugs found in EthRex and that:
D — custom chain: category which applies to specific genesis state (e.g. reth importing 0xef01 accounts)
D — synthetic state: category which happens where we attempt to overflow certain calculates (gas limits / base fees)
D — contained upstream: most/all of these seem to be part of networking/engine API? 
mainnet-glamsterdam: these are bugs found on latest devnet?

Checking if these categories match what I suspect they are 🙂

I would suggest to also, if not yet done, integrate execution-specs in the code review. Execution-specs is used to fill most/all (?) glamsterdam tests and is thus used as source of truth for the eip implementations. If the tool finds a bug there then we should check if this is specified in the EIP, and if this is not the case then it should be clarified because then the EIP has a blind spot

author: jochembrouwer
content: Did you contact EthRex regarding the found problems? And other clients also for the related problems flagged above? If not we should redirect this repo to them 😄

author: catwith1hat
content:
<@508125616940515329> I disclosed all the bugs in ethrex in private, so all the things found are fixed.

Your intuition of the categories are roughly correct. Except the synthetic state category, I'd describe as "the state transition function disagree on state that is unreachable from mainnet or custom chains (hence the term synthetic as opposed to organic)". 

I think that the audit already took into account the execution tests but I am not sure. At least one client has them embedded in the repo, I believe. But yes, having the tests handy is good. That said, the agent took upon itself to also verify all the more trivial opcodes. I assume that in some parts that replicated the execution-specs tests.

(Funny side note, in https://github.com/catwith1hat/evm-breaker/tree/master/items/035 the agent discovered the "OpDiffer" paper, which came out on the 16h of April.. and I started with this project somewhere on the 20th.)

author: catwith1hat
content: What's also interesting. I gave the agent access to my GitHub login via the `gh` tool, and told it to find me all the information about the glamsterdam tests by pointing it just to the ethereum/pm repo. It went and read all the meeting notes from the ACD* calls to figure out the different branches in the individual clients.

author: jochembrouwer
content: I wanted to propose to point it to forkcast but it seems it also found that 😄

author: catwith1hat
content: I explicitly pointed the agent to forkcast, because it's awesome 🙂

author: m0_ox
content: I took at look at the EIP-7928 (BALs) cases [here](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/pull/2834) and it looks like most seem to actually be covered already. That said, I added BAL expectations to these as guard-rails / sanity checks but as long as the clients are passing these they should've had test cases covered. Some of these did spark new cases though (I believe 5/18 of the reported cases there) and I extended the coverage a bit more. Thanks for flagging 👍🏽.

author: m0_ox
content: We will have to rebase the bal-devnet-7 branch once these land in `forks/amsterdam` as well so that the new tests propagate up to it but we'll take care of that part.
